coaching.coaching: Pin down AC3 grep window boundary conditions with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: AC3 grep window boundary conditions

coaching.coaching: Plan how verification command correctness for all ACs will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: verification command correctness for all ACs

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Widen the `grep -A2` window in AC3 to `grep -A5` (or use `grep -A10`) so the proving command robustly finds 'rebar review-code' anywhere in a reasonably-sized index entry.
evidence: The plan does not constrain how many lines after the heading the 'rebar review-code' text must appear, yet `grep -A2` only captures 2 lines, meaning a 3-line entry silently fails the check while satisfying the stated intent.
VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item (5): missing cardinality / window assertion; `grep -A2` is a fixed-window assumption not stated in the criterion.
findings.finding: AC3's proving command uses `grep -A2` (only 2 lines after the match), which silently fails if the implementation places 'rebar review-code' on the 3rd or later line after the `passing-code-review.md)**` anchor in README.md — a plausible formatting outcome for a multi-line index entry.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: `grep -A2 'passing-code-review.md)\*\*' docs/README.md | grep -q 'rebar review-code'`
scenarios: A well-formed README index entry with a heading line, a one-sentence description, and then the 'rebar review-code' mention on line 3 would pass human review but fail the AC grep, producing a false-failure.
Conversely, a future edit that moves the mention to line 4+ causes the AC to silently regress without anyone noticing.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -A2 'passing-code-review.md)\*\*' docs/README.md | grep -q 'rebar review-code'` with `grep -A5 'passing-code-review.md)\*\*' docs/README.md | grep -q 'rebar review-code'`

evidence: verify command `grep -q 'REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW' docs/passing-code-review.md && grep -q '## Preview the review locally' docs/passing-code-review.md` greps the unanchored word 'REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW":')

verify command `grep -q 'rebar review-code — optional local preview' docs/passing-code-review.md` greps the unanchored word 'rebar' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_rebar'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"rebar":')

verify command `grep -q 'preview the review locally with .rebar review-code' AGENTS.md` greps the unanchored word 'preview' — it substring-matches (e.g. 'review_preview'); anchor it (\b, ^$, or a quoted key like '"preview":')
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC1 and AC5 so they agree on the exact heading text: either change AC1's verify grep to `grep -q '## Preview the review locally before you push'` or correct AC5's claimed anchor to `#preview-the-review-locally`.
evidence: AC1 verify: `grep -q '## Preview the review locally' docs/passing-code-review.md`
AC5 text: 'GitHub-generated anchor `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push` matches the heading text'
Heading `## Preview the review locally` → GitHub anchor `#preview-the-review-locally` (not `-before-you-push`)
findings.finding: AC1 and AC5 imply conflicting heading texts. AC1's verify grep (`grep -q '## Preview the review locally'`) is consistent with the heading `## Preview the review locally`, which GitHub would render as anchor `#preview-the-review-locally`. AC5 asserts the anchor is `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push` and claims it 'matches the heading text' — which requires the heading to be `## Preview the review locally before you push`. An implementer following AC1's literal text will create a heading that produces the wrong anchor, causing AC5 to fail.
scenarios: Implementer writes heading `## Preview the review locally` (satisfying AC1 grep), then AC5's grep for `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push` fails because no such anchor or link string exists in the file.
Implementer writes the longer heading to satisfy AC5, but is confused because AC1 doesn't name the full heading text.
findings.suggested_fix: If the intended heading is `## Preview the review locally before you push`, update AC1's grep to `grep -q '## Preview the review locally before you push' docs/passing-code-review.md`. If the intended heading is the shorter form, update AC5's anchor to `#preview-the-review-locally` and its explanatory comment accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that the new 'Preview the review locally' section clearly distinguishes the disabled-by-default (empty output) vs. enabled (full four-pass) states so the local/Gerrit equivalence claim in the Context is accurate as written in the docs.
evidence: docs/llm-framework.md line 348: 'OFF by default (verify.enable_code_review / REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW), it returns an inert empty review_result when disabled'
Plan Context: 'rebar review-code runs the same four-pass reviewer the Gerrit bot casts LLM-Review with'
AC1 mentions REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW as an 'enabling requirement' but the Context still asserts unconditional equivalence
findings.finding: The Context asserts rebar review-code 'runs the same four-pass reviewer the Gerrit bot casts LLM-Review with', implying local and CI behavior are equivalent. The codebase shows review_code is OFF by default (verify.enable_code_review / REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW) and 'returns an inert empty review_result when disabled' (docs/llm-framework.md line 348). Without the env var set the local invocation produces no review output at all — not the same four-pass output the Gerrit bot produces. The plan's AC1 acknowledges REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW as an 'enabling requirement', but the Context premise of full equivalence is only true when the gate is enabled, and the new section teaches the tool without clearly anchoring the baseline (disabled = no output) vs. enabled (true four-pass) distinction.
findings.location: Context / Problem section — 'runs the same four-pass reviewer the Gerrit bot casts LLM-Review with'; also AC1 mentioning REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW
scenarios: An author reads the new 'Preview the review locally' section, runs rebar review-code without REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW=1, gets empty output, and thinks the Gerrit gate will also be a no-op — incorrect, the Gerrit bot has the gate enabled.
The new section teaches the command but a reader following only the intro cross-link (before reaching the enabling-requirement detail) believes the local run is always equivalent to the CI run.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Preview the review locally' section, lead with: 'rebar review-code is disabled by default; set REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW=1 (and install the [agents] extra) to run the same four-pass review the Gerrit bot runs. Without it the command returns empty output.' This makes the Context premise accurate: equivalence holds when the env var is set.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC1 and AC5 on the exact heading text: if the intended heading is `## Preview the review locally before you push`, update the Scope section and AC1's grep to use the full title; if the shorter form is intended, correct AC5's anchor to `#preview-the-review-locally`.
evidence: AC1 verify: `grep -q '## Preview the review locally' docs/passing-code-review.md` — matches heading `## Preview the review locally`
AC5 text: 'GitHub-generated anchor `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push` matches the heading text' — requires heading `## Preview the review locally before you push`
Scope section names the heading as 'Preview the review locally' (shorter form, no 'before you push')
GitHub anchor algorithm: spaces→hyphens, lowercase; `## Preview the review locally` → `#preview-the-review-locally`, not `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push`
findings.finding: AC1 greps for `## Preview the review locally` (Scope section also uses this shorter name), but AC5 asserts the GitHub-generated anchor is `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push`, which only GitHub produces from the longer heading `## Preview the review locally before you push`. These two specifications imply different heading texts, leaving the implementer uncertain which heading to write and which anchor the intro cross-link (AC5) should point to.
scenarios: Implementer writes heading `## Preview the review locally` (matching Scope wording and AC1 grep literal); AC5's grep for `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push` fails because the file contains no such anchor string and the intro link uses the wrong anchor.

Implementer writes heading `## Preview the review locally before you push` to satisfy AC5; AC1's grep still passes (substring match), but the heading diverges from the Scope description — a minor inconsistency that could mislead future editors.
findings.suggested_fix: Decide on one heading. If `## Preview the review locally before you push` (as AC5 implies): update Scope to name it that way and change AC1's grep to `grep -q '## Preview the review locally before you push' docs/passing-code-review.md`. If `## Preview the review locally` (as Scope implies): update AC5's anchor to `#preview-the-review-locally`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC1 and AC5 so both greps target the same full heading text: if the intended heading is '## Preview the review locally before you push', update AC1's grep to `grep -q '## Preview the review locally before you push' docs/passing-code-review.md`; if the intended heading is the shorter form, update AC5's claimed anchor to `#preview-the-review-locally`.
evidence: AC1 verify: `grep -q '## Preview the review locally' docs/passing-code-review.md`
AC5 text: 'GitHub-generated anchor `#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push` matches the heading text'
GitHub anchor derivation: heading '## Preview the review locally' → anchor '#preview-the-review-locally' (missing '-before-you-push')

GitHub anchor derivation: heading '## Preview the review locally before you push' → anchor '#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push' (satisfies AC5 but AC1 grep is only a prefix match, not a full-heading match)
findings.finding: AC1 and AC5 specify mutually inconsistent heading texts for the new section. AC1's verify grep targets '## Preview the review locally', which GitHub renders as anchor '#preview-the-review-locally'. AC5 asserts the anchor must be '#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push', which requires the heading '## Preview the review locally before you push'. An implementer satisfying AC1's literal grep will produce the wrong anchor, causing AC5 to fail; an implementer writing the longer heading satisfies AC5 but AC1's grep is only a substring match so both could pass — yet the plan claims the anchor 'matches the heading text', which is only true for the longer heading. This is a structural inconsistency in the plan's co-located verification protocol.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (`grep -q '## Preview the review locally'`) and AC5 (`grep -q '#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push'`)
scenarios: Implementer writes heading '## Preview the review locally' (satisfying AC1's substring grep), but AC5's grep for '#preview-the-review-locally-before-you-push' finds no match because the anchor is '#preview-the-review-locally' — AC5 fails.
Implementer writes the longer heading to satisfy AC5, but future maintainers changing the heading to the shorter form (matching AC1's grep) silently break the anchor link without any AC catching it.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC1's verify grep to `grep -q '## Preview the review locally before you push' docs/passing-code-review.md` to match the longer heading that AC5's anchor requires.

findings.finding: The Scope names 'loop diagram' as a sub-deliverable for docs/passing-code-review.md, but no AC verifies its presence — it is an in-scope content hunk without a verifying criterion.
findings.location: Scope: 'docs/passing-code-review.md (new "Preview the review locally" section + intro cross-link + loop diagram)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '- [ ] `docs/passing-code-review.md` new section contains a fenced-code loop diagram showing the `rebar review-code` → amend → re-run cycle (mirroring the `writing-a-passing-plan.md` diagram); verified by reading the rendered file.'

findings.finding: The 'loop diagram' sub-deliverable listed in Scope for docs/passing-code-review.md has no covering acceptance criterion, leaving it unverifiable and possibly unimplemented without detection.
findings.location: Scope — 'docs/passing-code-review.md (new "Preview the review locally" section + intro cross-link + loop diagram)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1: '…; the section includes the loop diagram illustrating the push→review→fix→push cycle; verified by reading the rendered file.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Escape the dot or use a literal backtick in AC4's grep pattern so it matches only the intended markdown-code form: `grep -q 'preview the review locally with \`rebar review-code' AGENTS.md` (or use `grep -F` with the literal backtick).
evidence: VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY item (1): substring false-positive — the `.` in the grep pattern is a BRE wildcard, not a literal character.
The intended character is a backtick (`) before 'rebar', but the pattern does not enforce it.
findings.finding: AC4's grep pattern uses an unescaped `.` (dot) before 'rebar', which in BRE matches any single character rather than the intended literal backtick, allowing a malformed string like 'preview the review locally with Xrebar review-code' to satisfy the AC.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4: `grep -q 'preview the review locally with .rebar review-code' AGENTS.md`
scenarios: Any single-character prefix before 'rebar review-code' — including a space, slash, or other punctuation — would satisfy the AC even if the AGENTS.md wording is not the intended markdown-code form.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `grep -q 'preview the review locally with .rebar review-code' AGENTS.md` with `grep -q 'preview the review locally with \`rebar review-code' AGENTS.md`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the 'Preview the review locally' section spec (and a corresponding AC) that the section must explain the consequence of each prerequisite being absent — e.g., what error or behavior to expect if `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` is not set and how to enable it, and how to install the `[agents]` extra if missing.
evidence: AC verifies only that the env var string appears in the file (`grep -q 'REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW'`), not that the docs explain the error or remediation when the var is unset.
No AC or plan prose specifies guidance for 'command exits with an error / does nothing because env var is missing' or 'agents extra not installed' scenarios.
A user following the new section who has not set `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` will hit a silent failure or cryptic error with no in-doc guidance on what to do.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the new 'Preview the review locally' section must mention `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` and the `[agents]` extra (verified by grep for presence), but does not specify that the section should explain what happens — or what error the user sees — when these prerequisites are absent, leaving the non-happy-path experience (the most likely first-run failure) unspecified.
findings.location: docs/passing-code-review.md — AC bullet 1: "incl. the `[agents]` extra and `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` enabling requirement"
scenarios: Developer reads the new section, runs `rebar review-code`, gets an error or no output because `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` is not set — doc gives no remediation hint.
Developer does not have `[agents]` extra installed — command fails with a dependency error not explained in the doc.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC 1 to verify that the section includes a note such as 'If the command does nothing or errors, ensure `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW=1` is set in your environment and the package is installed with `pip install rebar[agents]`.' — and add a grep-based AC that checks for this remediation text.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a requirement (and a corresponding AC grep) that the new 'Preview the review locally' section explicitly explains the silent-empty-output behavior when `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` is unset, and provides the exact command to enable it for a local run.
evidence: src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py: 'Disabled (the default — verify.enable_code_review off): returns a valid EMPTY review_result'
src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py line 115: summary='code-review capability disabled (verify.enable_code_review is off).'
docs/llm-framework.md line 348: 'OFF by default … returns an inert empty review_result when disabled'
AC1 verifies only that the string 'REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW' appears in the file — not that the silent-empty-output fallback is explained
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the new section must mention `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` as an 'enabling requirement', but no AC or plan prose specifies that the section must explain the fallback behavior when the var is absent. Code confirms (src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py) that without this var the command returns a silent, inert empty `review_result` with zero findings — indistinguishable to a naive reader from 'no issues found'. An author following the new on-ramp section who omits the env var will get empty output and no in-doc explanation of why, defeating the discoverability goal.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('incl. the `[agents]` extra and `REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW` enabling requirement'); src/rebar/llm/code_review/shim.py lines 6 and 115
scenarios: Developer reads the new 'Preview the review locally' section, runs `rebar review-code` without setting REBAR_VERIFY_ENABLE_CODE_REVIEW=1, gets empty JSON output with no findings, and incorrectly concludes there are no issues — the Gerrit bot (which force-enables the gate) may still BLOCK their change.
Developer sees empty output and thinks the tool is broken, not that it is intentionally inert when disabled.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
